questions.text: Two angles whose sum is 90 degrees are called:
questions.explanation: Complementary angles add up to 90 degrees.

questions.text: Two angles whose sum is 180 degrees are called:
questions.explanation: Supplementary angles add up to 180 degrees.

questions.text: If one angle of a linear pair is 70 degrees, the other angle is:
questions.explanation: Linear pair angles are supplementary: 180 - 70 = 110 degrees.

questions.text: Vertically opposite angles are always:
questions.explanation: When two lines intersect, vertically opposite angles are always equal.

questions.text: The complement of 35 degrees is:
questions.explanation: Complement = 90 - 35 = 55 degrees.

questions.text: The supplement of 75 degrees is:
questions.explanation: Supplement = 180 - 75 = 105 degrees.

questions.text: When two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, corresponding angles are:
questions.explanation: Corresponding angles are equal when lines are parallel.

questions.text: Alternate interior angles when two parallel lines are cut by a transversal are:
questions.explanation: Alternate interior angles are equal when lines are parallel.

questions.text: Co-interior angles (on the same side of transversal) between parallel lines are:
questions.explanation: Co-interior angles (also called same-side interior angles) add up to 180 degrees.

questions.text: The sum of all angles of a triangle is:
questions.explanation: The angle sum property of a triangle states that all three angles add up to 180 degrees.

questions.text: An angle greater than 90 degrees but less than 180 degrees is called:
questions.explanation: An obtuse angle is between 90 and 180 degrees.

questions.text: When two lines intersect, the number of pairs of vertically opposite angles formed is:
questions.explanation: Two intersecting lines form 4 angles and 2 pairs of vertically opposite angles.

questions.text: If a transversal cuts two lines and alternate interior angles are equal, the lines are:
questions.explanation: Equal alternate interior angles is a condition for two lines to be parallel.

questions.text: An exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of its:
questions.explanation: Exterior Angle Theorem: exterior angle = sum of the two non-adjacent (remote) interior angles.

questions.text: If angle A + angle B = 90 degrees, then angles A and B are:
questions.explanation: Two angles summing to 90 degrees are complementary.

questions.text: Lines that never meet, no matter how far extended, are called:
questions.explanation: Parallel lines never intersect and are always the same distance apart.

questions.text: A line that intersects two or more lines at distinct points is called a:
questions.explanation: A transversal is a line that crosses two or more other lines at different points.

questions.text: The measure of a straight angle is:
questions.explanation: A straight angle is formed by a straight line and measures 180 degrees.

questions.text: If two lines are perpendicular to the same line, they are:
questions.explanation: Two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel to each other.

questions.text: In a linear pair, the two angles are always:
questions.explanation: A linear pair is formed on a straight line, so the two angles always add to 180 degrees.

questions.text: Two supplementary angles are in ratio 1:2. Find the smaller angle:
questions.explanation: Let angles be x and 2x. x + 2x = 180, so x = 60 degrees.

questions.text: Two straight lines PQ and RS intersect at O. If angle POR = 40 degrees, find angle QOS:
questions.explanation: Angle QOS and angle POR are vertically opposite angles, so angle QOS = 40 degrees.

questions.text: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One co-interior angle is 115 degrees. The other co-interior angle is:
questions.explanation: Co-interior angles are supplementary: 180 - 115 = 65 degrees.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, angle A = 50 degrees and angle B = 70 degrees. Find angle C:
questions.explanation: Angle C = 180 - 50 - 70 = 60 degrees.

questions.text: An exterior angle of a triangle is 110 degrees. One non-adjacent interior angle is 60 degrees. Find the other:
questions.explanation: Exterior angle = sum of non-adjacent interior angles: 110 = 60 + x, x = 50 degrees.

questions.text: The complement of an angle is 25 degrees. Find the supplement of the same angle:
questions.explanation: Angle = 90 - 25 = 65 degrees. Supplement = 180 - 65 = 115 degrees.

questions.text: Two lines AB and CD intersect at O. If angle AOC = 3x - 10 degrees and angle BOD = 2x + 20 degrees, find x:
questions.explanation: Vertically opposite angles are equal: 3x - 10 = 2x + 20, so x = 30.

questions.text: AB || CD. If the alternate interior angle to angle APQ is 75 degrees, then angle APQ is:
questions.explanation: Alternate interior angles are equal when lines are parallel, so angle APQ = 75 degrees.

questions.text: In triangle PQR, the exterior angle at R is 120 degrees. If angle P = 50 degrees, find angle Q:
questions.explanation: Exterior angle = angle P + angle Q: 120 = 50 + angle Q, so angle Q = 70 degrees.

questions.text: Two complementary angles are in ratio 2:3. Find the larger angle:
questions.explanation: Let angles be 2x and 3x. 2x + 3x = 90, x = 18. Larger angle = 3 x 18 = 54 degrees.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, angle B - angle C = 30 degrees and angle A = 60 degrees. Find angle B:
questions.explanation: angle B + angle C = 120 (since angle A = 60). angle B - angle C = 30. Adding: 2 angle B = 150, angle B = 75 degrees.

questions.text: Two angles of a triangle are in ratio 3:4. The third angle is 40 degrees. Find the largest of the three angles:
questions.explanation: 3x + 4x + 40 = 180, 7x = 140, x = 20. Angles are 60 and 80. Largest = 80 degrees.

questions.text: AB || CD. A transversal cuts AB at P and CD at Q. Angle APQ = 5x - 10 degrees and angle PQD = 3x + 20 degrees (alternate interior angles). Find x:
questions.explanation: Alternate interior angles are equal: 5x - 10 = 3x + 20, 2x = 30, x = 15.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, the bisectors of angles B and C meet at point O. If angle A = 70 degrees, find angle BOC:
questions.explanation: angle BOC = 90 + (angle A / 2) = 90 + 35 = 125 degrees.

questions.text: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. A co-interior angle pair is in ratio 2:7. Find the smaller angle:
questions.explanation: 2x + 7x = 180, 9x = 180, x = 20. Smaller angle = 2 x 20 = 40 degrees.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, the angles are in ratio 2:3:4. Find the largest angle:
questions.explanation: 2x + 3x + 4x = 180, 9x = 180, x = 20. Largest angle = 4 x 20 = 80 degrees.

questions.text: Find the complement of 47 degrees.
questions.explanation: Complement = 90 - 47 = 43 degrees.

questions.text: Find the supplement of 123 degrees.
questions.explanation: Supplement = 180 - 123 = 57 degrees.

questions.text: Two lines intersect at a point. One angle is 3 times the adjacent angle. Find the smaller angle (in degrees).
questions.explanation: Adjacent angles form a linear pair: x + 3x = 180, 4x = 180, x = 45 degrees.

questions.text: In triangle PQR, angle P = 3 times angle Q and angle R = 2 times angle Q. Find angle Q (in degrees).
AB || CD. A transversal makes angle APQ = 130 degrees with AB. Find the co-interior angle PQC (in degrees).
questions.explanation: Co-interior angles are supplementary: 180 - 130 = 50 degrees.

questions.text: An exterior angle of a triangle is 115 degrees. One non-adjacent interior angle is 55 degrees. Find the other non-adjacent interior angle (in degrees).
questions.explanation: Exterior angle = sum of non-adjacent interior angles: 115 = 55 + x, x = 60 degrees.

questions.text: Two supplementary angles differ by 30 degrees. Find the larger angle (in degrees).
questions.explanation: Let angles be x and x-30. x + (x-30) = 180, 2x = 210, x = 105 degrees.

questions.text: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One alternate exterior angle is 72 degrees. Find the other alternate exterior angle (in degrees).
questions.explanation: Alternate exterior angles are equal when lines are parallel: 72 degrees.

questions.text: In triangle ABC, angle A : angle B : angle C = 2:3:4. Find the largest angle (in degrees).
questions.explanation: 2x + 3x + 4x = 180, x = 20. Largest angle = 4 x 20 = 80 degrees.

questions.text: The angles of a triangle are (x+10) degrees, (2x-30) degrees and (3x+20) degrees. Find x.

cases.case_text: A city surveyor is mapping two main parallel roads AB and CD. A diagonal road (transversal) PQ crosses both roads. At the intersection with road AB, the angle APQ measures 70 degrees. The traffic department needs to find all the angles at both intersections to design proper road signs, speed limits, and traffic flow directions.

subparts.text: The alternate interior angle to angle APQ at the intersection with road CD is:
subparts.explanation: Alternate interior angles are equal when lines are parallel: 70 degrees.

subparts.text: The co-interior angle to angle APQ on the same side at road CD is:
Find the vertically opposite angle to angle APQ at the intersection with road AB (in degrees).
subparts.explanation: Vertically opposite angles are equal: 70 degrees.

cases.case_text: In a triangular park ABC, a gardener observes that angle A = 50 degrees and angle B = 65 degrees. The path along side BC is extended beyond C to a point D, forming an exterior angle ACD. The gardener wants to fence the entire park and needs to know all angles to calculate the correct lengths and positions of the fence posts and gate openings.

subparts.text: Find the interior angle C of the triangular park (in degrees):
The exterior angle ACD at C is:
subparts.explanation: Exterior angle ACD = angle A + angle B = 50 + 65 = 115 degrees.

subparts.text: If the park is redesigned so that angle A = angle B and angle C = 80 degrees, find angle A (in degrees).
subparts.explanation: angle A + angle A + 80 = 180, 2 angle A = 100, angle A = 50 degrees.
